content.problemStatement:
An array is called sortable if it can be sorted in non-descending order using the following operations several times (possibly 000 times):


Choose index iii, and multiply AiA_iAi‚Äč by ‚ąí1-1‚ąí1.
For example, [3,2,1,1,2][3, 2, 1, 1, 2][3,2,1,1,2] can form [‚ąí3,‚ąí2,1,1,2][-3, -2, 1, 1, 2][‚ąí3,‚ąí2,1,1,2] which is sorted.

You are given an array of NNN positive integers - [A1,A2,‚Ä¶,AN][A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N][A1‚Äč,A2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äč]. Find the number of pairs LLL and RRR such that


1‚Č§L‚Č§R‚Č§N1 \le L \le R \le N1‚Č§L‚Č§R‚Č§N
[AL,AL+1,‚Ä¶,AR][A_L, A_{L + 1}, \ldots, A_R][AL‚Äč,AL+1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,AR‚Äč] is sortable.
In short, the problem asks to find the number of sortable subarrays of AAA.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer NNN - the size of the array.
The second line contains NNN integers - A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1‚Äč,A2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äč.


Output Format
Output the number of sortable subarrays of AAA.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
There are a total of 101010 subarrays. Only (L,R)=(1,4)(L, R) = (1, 4)(L,R)=(1,4) and (L,R)=(2,4)(L, R) = (2, 4)(L,R)=(2,4) are not sortable. All the other 888 are sortable
